text:
NSI Daily Market Report 14/01/2026
Best Regards:
MR. DEEPESH MAJIGAMKAR
Mob:- + 91 9730358718 (whatsapp) // +91 9156366155
Teams/Skype:deepeshmajigamkar@hotmail.com
Email:
chartering@varitasnavigation.com
Website:-
www.varitasnavigation.com
From:
Nautical Supply International (NSI) DMCC <carolina@nauticalsupply.co.uk>
Sent:
14 January 2026 17:42
To:
Deepesh Majigamkar <chartering@varitasnavigation.com>
Subject:
NSI Daily Market Report 14/01/2026
Hi Deepesh
We have had a slight correction this morning following yesterday’s rises.
The API has estimated that US crude oil inventories increased by 5.27 million barrels in the week ending on the 7th of January, after dropping by 2.8 million barrels in the week prior to that. The DoE has also reported that crude oil inventories in the Strategic Petroleum Reserve has reached 413.7 million barrels in the week ending on the 9th of January, rising by 200,000 barrels. Over the course of last year, crude inventories have been steadily building momentum as the current administration works to rebuild the national stockpile until it manages to reach former-glory levels. However, according to the latest EIA data, production in the United States had dropped to 13.811 million barrels per day during the week of January 2nd; although this is down from 13.827 million barrels per day in the week prior, production is 248,000 barrels per day more compared to figures last year during the same period of time.
Additional news surrounding the US seizure is that after the United States had captured Maduro, major state-owned Chinese and Russian companies had started to doubt their claims over billions of Venezuelan oil barrels that had been made under deals, said the investment bank Morgan Stanley. The single biggest claim from a company that has operated in Venezuela is from China Petroleum & Chemical Corporation - also known as Sinopec - which under current agreements with PDVSA, the oil giant and top Asian refiner is entitled to as much as 2.8 billion of barrels, noted Morgan Stanley Research cited by Bloomberg. This is followed by Russia\'s Roszarubezhneft, with claims on Venezuelan oil worth 2.3 billion barrels, estimated the investment bank, using data from Wood Mackenzie, which also mentioned that China\'s CNPC has claims over 1 billion barrels of Venezuelan oil reserves, that\'s followed by Chevron, who\'s entitled to 900 million barrels. Moreover, estimates from Morgan Stanley showed that other companies - including Eni, Repsol, and other small Venezuelan firms - also have claims that reaches hundreds of millions of barrels. The investment bank had noted that “The crucial question is what will happen with Venezuela’s production from here. This remains hard to forecast... Over the medium term, however, risks to production are clearly to the upside, at least from a resource and technical perspective.”
Denmark’s prime minister Mette Frederiksen had issued a warning earlier this month that Trump is now seriously planning to take over Greenland, though this may not come as a surprise for some, since earlier last year he had wanted to buy Greenland in order to gain access to its abundant resources, a large one being oil. The successful seizure in Venezuela appears to have boosted Trump\'s confidence, sparking worries over the extent of his power and how he may potentially use it, particularly due to the fact that after Trump came into office, he\'s demonstrated on several occasions that he\'s willing to go to extreme lengths to achieve his goals. Notably, Greenland is the largest island in the world, the Arctic territory spanning 2.17 million square kilometres - or 836,000 square miles - but almost 80% of its land is covered in ice, meaning that the land that\'s ice-free is roughly 410,450 square kilometres. The Arctic holds significant deposits of rare earth elements, cobalt, nickel, and other metals that are all crucial for making things such as batteries, EVs, and defence technology. The vast amount of untapped critical minerals reserves is part of the reason why Trump is determined to get his hands on Greenland, as it is no secret at this point that the US president is highly motivated by crude, seeking to "drill, baby, drill" as much oil as he can, on top of wanting the US to reduce its reliance on Chinese imports. Considering the fact that climate change is melting the ice and permafrost in the Arctic, further land exploration to attain the critical minerals has become more accessible, which is undoubtedly very alluring for Trump, whilst the increasing physical access for mining has additionally provided valuable opportunities for nations like Greenland, Russia, and Canada. However, the melting ice and thawing ground in that region has also created new challenges, such as destabilizing infrastructure, hastening the complex geopolitical race for these resources which are crucial for technology and the energy sector, as well as overall environmental risks. In the past, we have seen the damage of oil spills caused by thawing permafrost has had on the climate and ecosystems. Therefore, it is safe to assume that the pollution from mining Greenland\'s resources, processing them, and the fuel used in transportation, will significantly threaten the surrounding fragile ecosystems and Indigenous communities. The oil industry continues to be one of leading causes of climate change, the effects it’s had on the world includes, but is not limited to, warming climates causing ice to melt as the atmosphere is being altered from greenhouse gases, resulting in rising water levels, and surging amounts of natural disasters. These problems impact oil trading one way or another - an example is that environmental concerns have led to protests against the crude oil, influencing people to find greener alternatives, and in some cases conducting physical blockades on some part of the trading process. Another example is that the rise in natural disasters caused by climate change have boosted the number of disruptions to production, imports/exports, etc... as refineries may need to temporarily shut-down, conduct maintenance on any damage, or postpone transportation dates. As a result, this has made many question about the efficacy of tapping into Greenland’s resources, whether it would all be sustainable, and if the added supply of crude/minerals is worth potentially worsening the climate crisis on top of the overall devastating implications that will likely result from mining. Despite these concerns, Trump doesn’t seem to be put off by any of it, making it clear throughout last year that he has no regards to global warming, calling the climate crisis a "joke". The president of United States had reiterated to reporters aboard Air Force One earlier this month, that the United States needs "Greenland from the standpoint of national security, and Denmark is not going to be able to do it", adding that “We need Greenland. … It’s so strategic right now. Greenland is covered with Russian and Chinese ships all over the place,” Trump told reporters aboard Air Force One. In response to the rising possibility of an invasion, Denmark\'s ambassador Christina Markus Lassen has expressed "deep concern" over the matter, stating that the "inviolability of borders are not up for negotiation". Moreover, Frederiksen had also mentioned that a US attack on a NATO ally would catalyse a much broader conflict, telling the Danish television network TV2 that "If the United States decides to militarily attack another NATO country, then everything would stop -- that includes NATO and therefore post-World War II security". Even though there\'s been no further indication that an invasion will occur, or any significant updates on the situation, the prospect of additional geopolitical conflict hasn’t helped the bearish sentiment in the markets. We will have to see if this political clash will be resolved peacefully, or if it will escalate further, which could be more devastating than the other ongoing conflicts since NATO may be factored in. Ideally, tensions will cease if both sides decide to resolve the matter at hand by negotiating, and hopefully agreeing, on a deal - or perhaps the United States will decide to completely back down on any plans to seize Greenland in order to avoid any possibility, in the worst case scenario, of a global war breaking out.
Aside from the recent head-liners, Reuters had reported that Trump threatened additional tariffs on India for its continued purchases and imports of Russian crude, further challenging India\'s diversification of oil supply amid unsteady prices and volatility in the markets. Trump had stated aboard Air Force One that “Modi is a good guy. He knew I was not happy, and it was important to make me happy... They do trade, and we can raise tariffs on them very quickly”. Since Trump came into office, he has been trying to pressure Russia\'s oil flows by placing tariffs and sanctions on its energy sector, including restrictions on countries that continue funding Russia\'s conflict with Ukraine, in order to squeeze Russia\'s main source of revenue for the war. The US president has been particularly harsh on India with punishments around the matter; in August 2025, Trump had placed an additional 25% tariff on all Indian imports after India had ignored his threats to discontinue its purchases of Russian crude. After the latest US sanctions on Russian majors Lukoil and Rosneft came into-affect at the end of last year, India had vowed to slowly reduce its imports of Russian energy commodities, with all but two refiners not placing their December delivery orders. Russia has been India\'s top crude supplier since India imports over 80% of its oil consumption; the sanction-related discount on Russian crude oil had caused India\'s energy import bill to drop significantly, making it the optimal choice. Regardless, due to India mainly relying on Russian imports to keep up with consumption demand, Indian executives had emphasised that purchases would be reduced gradually since the country will be prioritising its own energy security above everything else. India\'s import rates have stooped to its lowest level since the G7 had imposed a price cap on Russian oil shipments that were insured at Western companies, due to the culmination of India beginning to curb its Russian crude purchases in December, on top of the current looming threat of more tariffs from the United States. As a result, according to the latest monthly update on Russia energy exports from the Finnish Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air (CREA), exports to India have declined by 29% on the month. It should be noted that despite the additional tariffs on Russia, and Indian refiners making efforts to reduce their purchases, there wasn’t any significant impact on imports, based on Reuters calculations from mid-December. The daily rate of imports from Russia had averaged at 1.2 million barrels around the middle of December, down from 1.77 million barrels in November, before the latest US sanctions, meaning that even though imports had dropped month-on-month, this was far less than what analysts had forecasted. Meanwhile, crude oil exports from Russia to China had jumped by 23% in December compared to November, said CREA in its report, adding that China had hiked its purchases of Russian crude the most in December - particularly noting that purchases of ESPO had reached its highest level in four months - with total Russian oil exports to China rising 11%. The drawback of Indian purchases has caused Russian crude to mainly be directed to the East as China increases its volumes, the pivot keeping its oil flows relatively supported. For now, import flows are predicted to stay strong throughout January as India has switched to purchasing crude from smaller non-sanctioned Russian oil companies in place of the now-sanctioned oil majors, indicating that India is trying to tread more lightly, exerting more caution in order to stay in Trump\'s good graces as much as possible, at least until they found a way around the sanctions. Yet, the recent tariff threat on India suggests that Trump isn’t satisfied with India\'s efforts so far, pushing for a complete cut off from all Russian supply, leaving India in a precarious situation where it has to balance between keeping the country\'s energy security stable whilst trying to appease Trump.
